claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
circumstances: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to start with some basic information about your child or children.

**First question: How old is your child (or children)?**

This is important because the age determines the basic eligibility criteria:
- **Under 16**: Children automatically qualify (subject to other conditions)
- **16-19**: May still qualify if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally not eligible

Please tell me the age(s) of the child/children you're inquiring about.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, he's in the automatic qualification age category. Now let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Given that Alex is only 8 years old, I expect he's not in paid work at all, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't work at all - he's only 8! He's in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That makes sense - Alex is in primary school and not working.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, is he receiving Employment and Support Allowance (ESA), Universal Credit, or similar benefits in his own name?

Again, given his age, this is unlikely, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

This question relates to apprenticeships, which typically apply to older teenagers. Given that Alex is 8 and in primary school, I expect he's not in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - he's only 8 and still in primary school. He's nowhere near old enough for an apprenticeship!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You're absolutely right! I apologize for the repetitive questions - the assessment process requires me to go through each check systematically, even when some don't apply to young children like Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we're moving on to questions about **you** as the claimant. 

**Question: Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, OR
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months), OR
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (for up to 91 days), OR
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself and your family, OR
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources, OR
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside, OR
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** Most commonly, people simply normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! You meet the residency requirements as you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can demonstrate responsibility if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex**, OR
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - this includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents), OR
3. You pay maintenance for Alex under a court order or agreement

**Which of these applies to your situation with Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me - he lives with his mum. I'm not currently contributing financially to his upkeep either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. So Alex lives with his mum, and you're not currently contributing financially to his upkeep.

Based on what you've told me, you don't meet the responsibility requirement for claiming Child Benefit for Alex. To be eligible, you need to either:
- Live with the child, OR
- Contribute financially to their upkeep (at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount)

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Result**

**Result: You are NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit for Alex**

### **Why you're not eligible:**

While Alex himself qualifies as an eligible child (he's 8 years old, in school, and meets all the child-specific requirements), and you meet the UK residency requirements, **you don't meet the responsibility requirement** for claiming Child Benefit.

To claim Child Benefit, you must be responsible for the child, which means:
- **Either** living with the child, **OR**
- Contributing at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (this includes things like food, clothes, housing, pocket money, presents, or maintenance payments)

Since Alex lives with his mum and you're not currently contributing financially to his upkeep, you cannot claim Child Benefit for him.

### **Important information:**

**Alex's mum may be eligible** to claim Child Benefit if she meets the other requirements (UK residency, etc.), since she lives with Alex and is responsible for him.

If you start making regular financial contributions towards Alex's care (at least equal to the Child Benefit amount), or if Alex comes to live with you in the future, you could then become eligible to claim.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and meets the basic child qualification criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in an apprenticeship). The claimant meets the UK residency requirements as they normally live in the UK (Manchester). However, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement because they do not live with Alex (who lives with his mother) and they are not contributing financially to Alex's upkeep. To claim Child Benefit, a claimant must be responsible for the child, which means either living with them OR contributing at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care. Since neither condition is met, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who qualifies as an eligible child. The claimant meets residency requirements. However, the claimant cannot claim Child Benefit because they do not meet the responsibility requirement - they neither live with Alex nor contribute financially to his upkeep.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.guidance: You must be responsible for the child, which means either living with them OR paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.). This can include maintenance payments under court order.

To claim Child Benefit, a claimant must be responsible for the child, which means either living with them OR contributing at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care. Since neither condition is met, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
